sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, Mrs. Kelly Wood, a 44-year-old female, presented with hematuria, which she described as bright red urine, the color of cherry Kool-Aid, for the past six weeks, and a few episodes of frequent urination accompanied by severe pain, rating an 8 out of 10, which improved after passing two small stones last week.
She had also passed two small stones last week.
The patient is aware that she does not drink enough water and consumes more Mountain Dew than water.
Past medical history: The patient is established in our office and has no reported allergies.
She is taking Alavert 10 milligrams a day.
Review of systems: The patient denies flank pain and lower back tenderness.
Current medications: Alavert 10 milligrams a day.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has no flank pain.
The abdomen is normal.
The anti-genital exam and vaginal exam are normal.
Test Results: The renal ultrasound showed a 6mm left renal stone, non-obstructing, and a right kidney of 10 cm in length by 5 cm in width with no stones, masses, or hydronephrosis.
The left kidney is 10.8 cm in length and 5 cm in width with a 6mm non-obstructing stone.
The urine dipstick was negative for leukocytes, nitrates, protein, ketone, bilirubin, and glucose, with a yellow and clear color, urobilinogen 0.2, pH 5.5, and specific gravity 1.020.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has hematuria due to a non-obstructing left renal stone.
Problem list: Hematuria; Chronic urolithiasis; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I advised the patient to drink 125 ounces of water a day, avoid sugary drinks like Mountain Dew, and reduce her intake of sodium-rich and animal protein foods.
I also provided a handout with dietary recommendations and what to avoid.
I did not recommend surgical intervention at this time.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in a year, unless she experiences any further issues with bleeding or pain, in which case she can call the office for an earlier appointment.
